records.content: In assignment 1, two of team members interested in different factors affect students’academic performance. Therefore, when choosing the topic, our team decided to collect four datasets from Kaggle in the purpose of discovering how different factors influence students’ academic performance.  Although each dataset contains different variables and was collected from different populations, they all focus on the relationship between students’ study time and their academic performance. This allows us to test the same topic with multiple perspectives and datasets as evidence. Based on the datasets, we developed two different arguments to compare how different student lifestyles (strict versus chill lifestyles) impact on academic performance. Each argument will be supported by several substantiations that investigate specific factors such as study time, stress level, parental education, social time, etc., and different leisure activities.

hierarchy.lvl3: Dataset 1: Student lifestyle and academic performance
Dataset 1: Student lifestyle and academic performance

records.content:
The first dataset used in this project is about Student Lifestyle (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/steve1215rogg​/student​-lifestyle​-dataset), providing the information about how different factors in student lifestyle (such as students’ daily habits across study, extracurriculars, sleep, socializing, stress levels, CGPA, and physical activities) can influence their academic performance (represented by GPA) and student well-being. This dataset contains 2000 records and 8 variables. In this project, we decided to use variables such as study hours, social hours, physical activity, stress level, GPA. For data preprocessing, we first checked the dataset structure by using pandas.DataFrame.head (n=5) to preview the first five rows, basic descriptive statistics through the kaggle dataset column, then checking variable name, data types, and missing values. Variables that were not relevant to our research were deleted. Second, for clearer visualization and consistency between datasets with different grading systems (e.g., GPA, final grade), we encoded students grade variables into categorical variable called Grade Group. Instead of using fixed score thresholds, we used a percentile tertile grouping (pandas.qcut) to divide students grade into three approximately equal-sized groups: Low, Medium, High. This allows us to ensure comparable proportions of grading across datasets. Finally, instead of merging dataset, we used each dataset to answer specific substantiation within our broader argument. This approach allows us to build more coherent storyline and show consistent patterns, while avoiding incorrect comparison between datasets with different samples and measurement scales.

The second dataset uses gives us data for the effects of gaming on education performance. This dataset was found on kaggle (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/aiexplorer77​/gaming​-vs​-academic​-performance). The dataset has 8000 records and 14 variables and has information on many different things that have to do with gaming and education. The most important of these variables that we used in this project are: gaming hours, reaction time and grades. These are all floats. We noticed that in this dataset there were a few people that have grades above 100, to keep the way we handle grades throughout the project consistent we decided to filter these people out of the dataset. There was no further cleaning done to keep the dataset as complete as possible. This was also helped by the fact that there were no missing values in the dataset, which means that every record could be used to its full extent.

We also made an extra column, which we named: grade_category. This column has three different categories as strings for grades: fail, sufficient and good. These categories are based on the “grades” column and are used in the different visualisation to easily show a person’s performance.

For the third dataset we chose the student alcohol consumption dataset from Kaggle (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/uciml​/student​-alcohol​-consumption). This is a fairly broad dataset because it consists of 33 columns and 393 rows. The columns vary from the age of the student to the job of their parents there is truly a lot that can be compared using this dataset. For our research we mainly focus on the columns: Dalc, Walc, G3 and studytime. It is also important to mention that this dataset consists of two sub-datasets, student-math and student-Portuguese, these sub-datasets consist of the same 33 columns and 393 rows. The only difference between the two, is that one of them measures everything for math students and the other for Portuguese language students. We decided to drop all the columns that are irrelevant for our research, thus only the ones named above remain. This way generating the visualisations will go a little quicker than before. After this we decided that we need to generalise the grades, this needs to be done because in Portugal they are used to grade from 1 to 20 instead of 1 to 10, which means that we needed to divide the final grade columns of both the math and Portuguese datasets by two. As last we decided to make a new column called ‘Alcohol’ to generalise alcohol consumption over the entire week, we did that by adding the columns workday alcohol (Walc) and weekend alcohol (Dalc) together and dividing the new number by two, so we remain with a 1 to 5 scale. We created this new column to make visualisations of the alcohol intensity more general and less complex for the user while also making generating the visualisations less difficult for us.

hierarchy.lvl3: Dataset 4: Student Habits vs Academic Performance
Dataset 4: Student Habits vs Academic Performance

records.content:
The last dataset that we utilized is “Student Habits vs Academic Performance” (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/jayaantanaath​/student​-habits​-vs​-academic​-performance​/data), containing habit-related variables such as sleep hours, diet quality, parttime job participation, etc. beside demographics records and their according exam grade, which represent academic performance as mentioned. Overall, this dataset has 1000 cases in total and 16 variables. Variables from this dataset that are being used in this study includes gender, study hour, exam grade, attendance, xxx.

To preprocess this dataset, we first removed duplicate data and those that are irrelevant. Then, we filtered out outliers by applying data onto the interquartile range (IQR); outliers removed were the values that lie outside [Q1 - 1.5IQR, Q3 + 1.5IQR], with IQR = Q3 - Q1. Lastly, we dealt with both numerical and categorical missing values in the dataset. Specifically, for 10 numerical variables, we replaced the spare space with the median value, while for the values from 6 categorical (string) variables, we filled in with mode.

What factors help improve academic performance has always been a crucial question of generations of students and also researchers in the education field (e.g. 

Adelantado-Renau et al. (2019), 

Singh et al. (2018), 

Camacho-Morles et al. (2021)).

In some perspectives, a successful study method is investing most of study time per day without any distraction from social activities, gaming or entertainment. Previous study shows that study habits, skills, and attitudes can predict student academic performance, meaning that having serious learning behaviors for everyday plays an important role in grades (

Credé & Kuncel (2008)).

However, nowadays, student life is not just studying. They also spent time socializing and participating in other physical or extracurricular activities. Studying in a strict way might not be the most effective method to acquire high scores. 

Barbosa et al. (2020) found that sports participation may benefit students’ academic achievement. Besides, according to 

Gilman et al. (2003) findings, participating in extracurricular activities in a structured way can also enhance school performance of students.

Therefore, this data story aims to investigate whether living chill or living strictly would be more beneficial to students’ study, and the role of external variables such as stress level and parental supervision in the relationship between type of lifestyle and academic performance. Four datasets shared on Kaggle were used to structure the story: “Student lifestyle dataset” (

Gilman et al. (2003)


https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/steve1215rogg​/student​-lifestyle​-dataset), “Gaming vs Academic Performance” (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/aiexplorer77​/gaming​-vs​-academic​-performance), “Student Alcohol Consumption” (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/uciml​/student​-alcohol​-consumption) and “Student Habits vs Academic Performance” (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/jayaantanaath​/student​-habits​-vs​-academic​-performance).
Having a strict lifestyle, such as focusing more on studying and reducing leisure activities, may improve students’ academic performance.

Students spending more time studying tend to achieve higher academic performance.

%run plots/study_time_plot.ipynb

Four datasets show the same patterns, in which students in the high grade group tend to spend more time studying than students in the medium and low grease group. In the dataset Student Alcohol Consumption, students who study Math will have higher study time than those who study Portuguese. Overall, even though the datasets come from different samples and use different grading systems, the consistent pattern indicates that academic performance is strongly correlated with higher academic performance.

For this sub-argument, we used bar charts to show the comparison of the average study time across low, medium, and high grade groups. The length of the bar and the position encode the average study time, helping the audience have a clear insight about the comparison. In the Student Alcohol Consumption dataset, we also used multiple charts with different color coding for Math and Portuguese to compare how the study time in different subjects affects their final grade. This approach allows us to indicate the consistent pattern across datasets while avoiding misleading comparison between different grading systems and measurement scales.

Student with better academic discipline tend to perform better in academic performance.

%run plots/attendance_plot.ipynb

The dataset gaming and student habit, which uses attendance percentage, show that students in high grade groups tend to have slightly higher average attendance and more academic discipline. The alcohol consumption dataset, which uses the opposite measurement - absence level, shows that students who achieve higher grades consistently have fewest average absences, while those in low and medium grade groups tend to be absent more often. Moreover, students studying Math tend to be absent more than students studying Portuguese.

For this substantiation, we used bar charts as academic percentage and absence level are numerical variables. The x-axis indicates students grade group, while y-axis shows the average attendance or absence level. In the Student Alcohol Consumption dataset, Math students are shown in different coding color with Portuguese students. Color is used as categorical encoding, helping audience distinguish whether the absence pattern differs between two subjects. This allows audience to see the comparison whether students with higher academic performance also show stronger academic discipline (high attendance and low absence).

Students who get higher grades tend to combine intensive study time with extra classes

%run plots/extra_class_plot.ipynb

In this substantiation, the line chart was used to show the academic investment represented by paid extra classes. The visualization indicates that the relationship between extra classes and study time lead to higher grades of students. Students who combine intensive study time with additional classes often achieve higher grades. However, the pattern does not show the same regarding Math and Portuguese. When it comes to Math students, those who joined academic support classes tend to have gradually increased grades even if they have low or high study time. In contrast, Math students who do not attend extra classes will have lower grades if they spend less time studying, but higher grades when they invest enough time for studying. Therefore, whether math students attend extra classes or spend more time studying may not fully explain their grades; their performance may also depend on problem-solving skills, learning strategies, and conceptual understanding ( 

Uyandıran & Tarım (2024), 

TURGUT (2021)). In terms of Portuguese, students who spend more time studying without extra classes tend to achieve higher grades than those who combine both of them. This difference lies in the nature of the subject. Mathematics requires teaching and various learning methods from the teacher (

Alegre-Ansuategui et al. (2017),  

Uyandıran & Tarım (2024)), while with foreign languages, self-study and practice will be more effective in improving students’ language skills and vocabulary ( 

Nakata (2016), 

Zhang & Zou (2022)).

The line charts are used to describe the relationship between combined study time and extra classes and average final grade of students. For both charts, the final grade is interval variable (y-axis), while study time is ordinal (x-axis). The two colored lines show students who attended extra classes and those who did not attend, coding as the legend of the graph. This design is useful because it shows the difference between extra-class groups, and the patterns change across different levels of study time.

Despite may affect mental wellbeing, strictly follow intense study schedule brings fruitful result

%run plots/pers1_subarg4.ipynb

Strictly living students experience moderate to high stress, but also achieve higher GPA, specifically a 0.2 gap for high stress and 0.1 gap for moderate

Because GPA has the maximum of 4 and is the average of 100+ ECTs, a difference of 0.1 is already notable

The clustered columns chart is chosen to illustrate data for the argument because there are 2 main variables: average GPA (ratio)and stress level (categorical), which is grouped into 2 groups of “Strict” and “Chill”. This type of chart helps show the correlation of grade and stress for each group. Additionally, aligning the 2 groups side-by-side assist comparison. Different shades of red are used to represent stress levels, bringing the necessary contrast for readers to easily notice levels of stress: the more intense the red shade, the higher the stress level.
The balanced lifestyle is about combining studying and leisure activities. In this part we will show how alcohol consumption and gaming impact education performance.

%run plots/alcohol_consumption_plot1.ipynb



The datasets student-math and student_portuguese, both part of the alcohol consumption dataset, show a downward trend in the intensity of drinking, shown on the x-axis, and the percentage of students who participates in drinking that often, shown on the y-axis. A high percentage of the students don’t drink at all (1 on the x-axis) and a very small percentage drinks everyday or almost every day (4.5-5 on the x-axis). This graph gives a clear illustration of the distribution of drinking intensity among students in these datasets. this bar-chart of the datasets also shows that there is no clear correlation between the course, math or Portuguese, and the percentage of students who participate at drinking at a certain intensity.

The primary mark used in this visualization is area, this is represented by rectangular bars. This mark was chosen because bar-charts are highly effective at showing proportions and frequencies due to it being represented by the length and area of each bar on the chart. We didn’t use point or line marks because these are mostly relevant for visualisations that represent single observations or continuous trends which isn’t the case for us.

As visual channel we mainly used position for the x- and y-axis which range from 1 to 5 and 0 to 50 percent respectively. This is done because numerical values from 1 to 5 are very easy to interpret for users and percentages are great at representing quantitative comparison. in addition, we also used the channels size and color. Size is encoded by the height of the bar that can be seen on the visualization and color is encoded by giving both datasets (math and Portuguese) a different color to make them easily distinguishable for the user. lastly, we didn’t use other channels such as value, texture, orientation, and shape because they would only over complexify the visualization and wouldn’t add additional meaningfulness for the user.

The visualization contains several data types, it primarily consists of ordinal data, this is because the alcohol consumption is measured in levels that can be ordered from 1 to 5 to represent ordered categories ranging from low to high alcohol consumption. This visualization also uses nominal and ratio data, the nominal data is represented by the labels math and Portuguese in de visualization in addition of different colors, the ratio data can be found on the y-axis where we used percentages to represent the bar length. Percentages are ratio data, because they have a meaningful zero point and allow comparisons.

%run plots/alcohol_consumption_plot.ipynb



The datasets student-math and student_portuguese, also show a very subtle trend when it comes to drinking intensity, here also shown on the x-axis, and the final grade at either math or Portuguese, shown on the y-axis. The medians of the final grade related to the alcohol intensity tend to drop slightly when the intensity increases. This is mostly the case at the Portuguese dataset, while we see the median drop from a 6.5 to a 5.0 when the drinking intensity increases from 1 to 5. At the math dataset on the other hand, we see a slight decline of the median from a 5.5 to a 4.0 when the intensity increases from 1 to 3, but after the median increases again to a 5.5 at intensity 5. This can be due to lack of observations for the 4 and 5 intensity in the student math dataset or it can be due to a less powerfull impact on grades when we consider a higher alcohol intensity and maths.

Spending more time gaming will reduce grades, but may improve response times which in turn will improve eduaction performance.

%run plots/gaming_stats_3d.ipynb



%run plots/gaming_response_time.ipynb



The two graphs suggest that reaction time does not appear to have a strong influence on grades, because the data points do not show a clear or consistent pattern. In contrast, the amount of gaming seems to be more strongly related to grades, since the trend in that graph is more noticeable. Taken together, this means our results do not support the idea that reaction time is an important predictor of grades in this dataset. That is interesting because it seems to contradict 

Prabu Kumar et al. (2020), who reports a different relationship between these factors and academic performance. The paper suggests that reaction time has a positive impact on educational performance because faster reaction times may reflect quicker information processing, better attentional control, or more efficient cognitive functioning, which can support learning and test performance.

The visualizations were designed to show the relationship between grades, reaction time, and gaming hours as clearly as possible. In the first figure, a 3D scatter plot is used so that all three variables can be displayed simultaneously: grades on one axis, reaction time on another, and gaming hours on the third. The axes are explicitly labeled, including the note that lower reaction times mean faster responses. Data points are colored by grade category using an intuitive traffic‑light palette (green for good, orange for sufficient, red for fail), which makes differences in performance easy to recognize at a glance and also keeps the categories visually consistent across figures. The amount of data points is reduced to improve visibility by taking a sample of 500 of the rows. This keeps hte same results, but make the graph more legible.

The second figure complements this view by combining several layers in a 2D layout. The central scatter plot shows grades against reaction time, with point size encoding gaming hours so that the third variable is still visible without requiring 3D rotation. Above this scatter, violin plots summarize the distribution of gaming hours for the different grade categories, making it easier to compare typical gaming behaviour between high‑ and low‑performing students rather than focusing on individual outliers. On the right, boxplots of grades by category display medians and variability, reinforcing the separation between good, sufficient, and failing groups. The same color scheme as in the first plot is reused so that the legend only needs to be learned once and the eye can move between figures without confusion.

For this sub-argument, we used bar charts to show the comparison of the average study time across low, medium, and high grade groups. The length of the bar and the position encode the average study time, helping the audience have a clear insight about the comparison. In the Student Alcohol Consumption dataset, we also used multiple charts with different color coding for Math and Portuguese to compare how the study time in different subjects affects their final grade. This approach allows us to indicate the consistent pattern across datasets while avoiding misleading comparison between different grading systems and measurement scales.

For this substantiation, we used bar charts as academic percentage and absence level are numerical variables. The x-axis indicates students grade group, while y-axis shows the average attendance or absence level. In the Student Alcohol Consumption dataset, Math students are shown in different coding color with Portuguese students. Color is used as categorical encoding, helping audience distinguish whether the absence pattern differs between two subjects. This allows audience to see the comparison whether students with higher academic performance also show stronger academic discipline (high attendance and low absence).

The line charts are used to describe the relationship between combined study time and extra classes and average final grade of students. For both charts, the final grade is interval variable (y-axis), while study time is ordinal (x-axis). The two colored lines show students who attended extra classes and those who did not attend, coding as the legend of the graph. This design is useful because it shows the difference between extra-class groups, and the patterns change across different levels of study time.

The clustered columns chart is chosen to illustrate data for the argument because there are 2 main variables: average GPA (ratio)and stress level (categorical), which is grouped into 2 groups of “Strict” and “Chill”. This type of chart helps show the correlation of grade and stress for each group. Additionally, aligning the 2 groups side-by-side assist comparison. Different shades of red are used to represent stress levels, bringing the necessary contrast for readers to easily notice levels of stress: the more intense the red shade, the higher the stress level.

The primary mark used in this visualization is area, this is represented by rectangular bars. This mark was chosen because bar-charts are highly effective at showing proportions and frequencies due to it being represented by the length and area of each bar on the chart. We didn’t use point or line marks because these are mostly relevant for visualisations that represent single observations or continuous trends which isn’t the case for us.

As visual channel we mainly used position for the x- and y-axis which range from 1 to 5 and 0 to 50 percent respectively. This is done because numerical values from 1 to 5 are very easy to interpret for users and percentages are great at representing quantitative comparison. in addition, we also used the channels size and color. Size is encoded by the height of the bar that can be seen on the visualization and color is encoded by giving both datasets (math and Portuguese) a different color to make them easily distinguishable for the user. lastly, we didn’t use other channels such as value, texture, orientation, and shape because they would only over complexify the visualization and wouldn’t add additional meaningfulness for the user.

The visualization contains several data types, it primarily consists of ordinal data, this is because the alcohol consumption is measured in levels that can be ordered from 1 to 5 to represent ordered categories ranging from low to high alcohol consumption. This visualization also uses nominal and ratio data, the nominal data is represented by the labels math and Portuguese in de visualization in addition of different colors, the ratio data can be found on the y-axis where we used percentages to represent the bar length. Percentages are ratio data, because they have a meaningful zero point and allow comparisons.

The visualizations were designed to show the relationship between grades, reaction time, and gaming hours as clearly as possible. In the first figure, a 3D scatter plot is used so that all three variables can be displayed simultaneously: grades on one axis, reaction time on another, and gaming hours on the third. The axes are explicitly labeled, including the note that lower reaction times mean faster responses. Data points are colored by grade category using an intuitive traffic‑light palette (green for good, orange for sufficient, red for fail), which makes differences in performance easy to recognize at a glance and also keeps the categories visually consistent across figures. The amount of data points is reduced to improve visibility by taking a sample of 500 of the rows. This keeps hte same results, but make the graph more legible.

The second figure complements this view by combining several layers in a 2D layout. The central scatter plot shows grades against reaction time, with point size encoding gaming hours so that the third variable is still visible without requiring 3D rotation. Above this scatter, violin plots summarize the distribution of gaming hours for the different grade categories, making it easier to compare typical gaming behaviour between high‑ and low‑performing students rather than focusing on individual outliers. On the right, boxplots of grades by category display medians and variability, reinforcing the separation between good, sufficient, and failing groups. The same color scheme as in the first plot is reused so that the legend only needs to be learned once and the eye can move between figures without confusion.

During this project, we first explored several datasets as a group and selected those related to education and lifestyle. These datasets allowed us to examine how different lifestyle factors may influence educational performance. Based on this, we aimed to answer the question of whether a strict lifestyle or a more balanced lifestyle leads to better academic outcomes. To investigate both perspectives, we created eight visualizations for each side, presenting evidence that either supports or challenges the effectiveness of each lifestyle.

Our findings suggest that a stricter lifestyle is generally associated with better educational performance. However, the data also indicates that this approach may lead to higher levels of stress. This highlights a trade-off between academic success and well-being, suggesting that while strict routines can improve performance, they may come at a personal cost.
